metadata.title: Using Topic Modeling to Research Student Diversity in Higher Education

abstract: Groups in computer-supported collaborative learning (CSCL) are characterized by diversity regarding several sociodemographic and task-related features (e.g., students differ with respect to their first language and their prior experience with online learning simultaneously). Understanding the effect of this multiattributional diversity on CSCL groups is necessary to inform the design of interventions aimed at managing CSCL group diversity. We used topic modeling to investigate how multiattributional diversity affects discussion topics and subsequent group performance. Twenty topics were derived by Latent Dirichlet Allocation and assigned to 17,720 forum posts by 342 groups of psychology freshmen. Multiattributional sociodemographic diversity was negatively related to the number of posts in two topics concerned with time management and feedback mechanisms in groups where multiattributional task-related diversity was also high. These topics were positively related to subsequent group performance ratings. Findings suggest that this combination of diversity presents a risk for group communication, potentially negatively impacting performance.

sections.paper_id: Voltmer et al. - 2024 - Using Topic Modeling to Research Student Diversity in Higher Education
paragraphs: Education systems around the world are facing two major trends. First, an increasing sociodemographic diversity within the student body, driven by migration, demographic changes, and the trend toward lifelong learning (see, for example, Schneller & Holmberg, 2014;Slowey & Schuetze, 2012;Stoessel et al., 2015). Second, the expansion of online learning (e.g., Lockee, 2021). The flexibility of online learning makes education more accessible to nontraditional students (e.g., in advanced career stages or with caregiving responsibilities), inherently increasing diversity in online learning. Often embedded within online learning are opportunities for computer-supported collaborative learning (CSCL). CSCL refers to virtual learning situations in which learners engage interactively to achieve a common educational goal (e.g., Francescato et al., 2006). In contrast to traditional face-to-face learning environments where groups collaborate in person, CSCL settings typically use asynchronous, text-based communication tools (e.g., message boards, instant messaging) that allow for interaction beyond the constraints of time and space (Lämsä et al., 2021), while at the same time presenting their own specific challenges to CSCL groups (Chang & Kang, 2016). There is a growing interest in understanding how student diversity influences CSCL interactions (e.g., Reich-Stiebert et al., 2023;Usher & Barak, 2020). Traditionally, research on diversity in CSCL has focused on singular characteristics, for instance, the role of first language (Usher & Barak, 2020) or cultural diversity (Lim & Liu, 2006). However, this approach may only partially capture the complexity of diversity, especially in contexts where students exhibit diversity across multiple demographic and educational dimensions simultaneously (e.g., Dutton et al., 2002;Schneller & Holmberg, 2014), leading to a fragmented understanding of how diversity impacts collaborative processes and outcomes in CSCL.

Therefore, we introduced the concept of multiattributional diversity, which denotes the aggregation of diversity characteristics like sex, age, first language (Voltmer et al., 2022). Our theoretical perspective on multiattributional diversity has been informed by theorizing and research in social and organizational psychology distinguishing between the two broader dimensions of task-related and sociodemographic diversity (e.g., Horwitz & Horwitz, 2007;van Knippenberg & Schippers, 2007). Task-related diversity is characterized by the differences among students in terms of their expertise, competencies, and functional roles directly relevant to the collaborative task. This may include differences in students' prior knowledge of the subject matter, previous experience with CSCL platforms, or familiarity with specific learning technologies. In contrast, sociodemographic group diversity encompasses differences among students that are not inherently related to the task but have the potential to influence the dynamics of collaboration. These differences may give rise to self-categorization and social identity processes, potentially triggering stereotypes or biases associated with specific sociodemographic characteristics.

Especially in asynchronous settings like CSCL, there is a tendency for groups to prioritize task-related over socioemotional exchange (e.g., Janssen et al., 2012;Kirschner et al., 2015). This prioritization might lead one to expect that task-related diversity would play a more significant role in shaping CSCL processes. On the other hand, the scarcity of individuating cues in asynchronous communication may amplify the salience of the few easily accessible sociodemographic attributes. Social psychological research in line with the Social Identity of Deindividuation Effects model shows that this lack of individuating information may increase the influence of superficial sociodemographic cues on impression formation and interactions (e.g., Flanagin et al., 2002;Spears et al., 2002). As a result, sociodemographic attributes may be more critical in shaping group dynamics, potentially leading to social categorization and the activation of stereotypes. Consequently, Reich-Stiebert et al. (2023), for example, found that first language diversity was negatively related to all group members' gains in self-efficacy. Moreover, and in line with the social psychological perspective on multiattributional diversity outlined above, Voltmer et al. (2022) found that multiattributional sociodemographic and taskrelated diversity had an interactive effect on group cohesion in CSCL: In groups featuring high task-related diversity, sociodemographic diversity was negatively related to group cohesion in the virtual networks of these groups. These findings suggest that groups featuring high multiattributional task-related diversity in combination with high multiattributional sociodemographic diversity face a high-risk constellation for their collaboration (Voltmer et al., 2024). In line with these findings, the CSCL literature suggests that sharing knowledge and expertise in virtual collaboration requires a common ground (Kirschner et al., 2008). High sociodemographic diversity may erode the interpersonal trust (van Dijk et al., 2016), especially in those groups most dependent on each other (i.e., when knowledge is unequally distributed among group members). Consequently, trust has been found to mediate the relationship between team diversity and team effectiveness in global virtual teams (Garrison et al., 2010;Pinjani & Palvia, 2013). However, the social psychological microdynamics underlying these interactive effects are not yet fully understood, and a more detailed investigation of these effects is necessary. The availability of data resulting from the asynchronous and text-based communication in CSCL presents a unique opportunity for learning analytics and diversity research. Automatic text mining techniques like topic modeling enable the analysis of large volumes of textual communications (e.g., Ferreira-Mello et al., 2019), providing deeper insights into the content of interactions within CSCL groups. Topic modeling encompasses a group of inductive techniques used as methods for discovery (Grimmer et al., 2022). Topic modeling, for example, is used to discover abstract topics within a collection of documents (Blei et al., 2003), thus offering a perspective on discourse content of CSCL groups. In educational research, topic modeling and other text mining techniques are currently often used to support the evaluation of students' performance (e.g., Ferreira-Mello et al., 2019) or to structure evaluative course feedback (e.g., Peñafiel et al., 2018). Despite the availability of data on CSCL groups, the application of topic modeling to investigate group processes in CSCL is relatively new. For instance, Sun et al. (2014) found that automatically extracted topics generally reflected the inquiry themes of the course, providing evidence for the potential of topic modeling research in CSCL. Dowell et al. (2019) used topic modeling to identify roles of group members, indicating the potentials for the analysis of social processes. In topic modeling, the topics emerge from the text rather than being defined a priori by researchers. Therefore, topic modeling allows for the discovery of social psychological dynamics hidden in the unstructured text corpus (Dowell et al., 2019).

In theory, diverse groups can benefit from a variety of perspectives resulting from their different backgrounds and experiences. In contrast to that, and in line with the social psychological perspective outlined above, CSCL groups with high multiattributional sociodemographic diversity may be at a disadvantage resulting from categorization processes. These categorization processes could result in the groups with high multiattributional diversity discussing fewer or different topics, which in turn may affect their performance in collaborative tasks. Topic modeling can help answer the still open question of how exactly CSCL group processes are affected by multiattributional diversity. To our knowledge, no research has investigated the role of multiattributional diversity on CSCL groups using text mining.

The contributions of our study are thus twofold. First, we use topic modeling to investigate which topics an automated algorithm identifies in a large corpus of interactions in CSCL groups. Second, we investigate the relationships between multiattributional diversity, the topics being discussed in these groups, and their performance on the CSCL task. Although this study is primarily exploratory in nature, based on these considerations and building on Voltmer et al. (2022Voltmer et al. ( , 2024)), we hypothesize the following:

Hypothesis 1 (H1): Multiattributional diversity is related to the topics being discussed in CSCL groups. We hypothesize that multiattributional sociodemographic and task-related diversity have an interactive effect, that is, multiattributional sociodemographic diversity has a negative effect on certain topics in groups with high task-related diversity.
Hypothesis 2 (H2): The prevalence of certain topics is related to group performance on the CSCL task.
Hypothesis 3 (H3): Topic prevalence partially mediates the relationship between multiattributional diversity and group performance.

sections.paper_id: Voltmer et al. - 2024 - Using Topic Modeling to Research Student Diversity in Higher Education
paragraphs: We used data from a larger-scale project on student diversity in CSCL (Stürmer et al., 2020). The project was conducted in the context of a mandatory course on scientific reading and writing. In this course, freshmen pursuing a B.Sc. in psychology were assigned a 9-week asynchronous CSCL task. During the course, students had to collaboratively summarize a psychological research paper. Two thousand eight hundred forty-four students were randomly assigned to 357 groups consisting of eight students. The data used for the present analyses were obtained from 342 CSCL groups with students completing the course and voluntarily participating in the self-report questionnaires. On average, four students separately completed self-report questionnaires. In line with the general student population in this specific institution's psychology program, 74% of the students were female, 46% of the students were 30 years or older, and 50% were part-time students. 14% reported German not being their first language. More than half of the students (52%) indicated a lower self-perceived socioeconomic status. Regarding task-related diversity, 22% participants had no previous online learning experience and 16% no experience reading scientific literature. Furthermore, 39% were second degree students and 2% even repeated the present course.

On first course login, students provided informed consent for the present study. Students created four separate wikis containing separate summaries of the research article's introduction, methods, results, and discussion sections. Questions guiding the summaries were provided to the students. For their collaboration, students were provided forums in the Moodle environment and instructed to complete all assigned tasks using Moodle communication tools. In the forums, students wrote on average 51.8 (SD = 35.2, Mdn = 45, range 2-184) posts per group and 13.6 posts (SD = 11.2, Mdn = 10, range 1-81) per student.

paragraphs: Students completed a survey on sociodemographic characteristics before collaborating. These characteristics, namely gender (0 = male, 1 = female), age (0 = under 30 years, 1 = 30 years and older), language proficiency (0 = native German speaker, 1 = non-native German speaker), and perceived socioeconomic status (SES; coded 0 for lower and 1 for higher scores on MacArthur's 10-point subjective socioeconomic status scale, see Hoebel et al., 2015), have been highlighted as critical predictors of success in online learning before (e.g., Koch, 2006;Lee & Choi, 2011;Stoessel et al., 2015). To quantify diversity, we first calculated individual diversity scores for each sociodemographic attribute using Blau's diversity index (Blau, 1977) and then averaged these scores to obtain a composite measure of multiattributional sociodemographic diversity for each group. The resulting scores reflect the diverse sociodemographic background ranging from 0 (complete homogeneity) to 0.5 (maximum diversity).

paragraphs: In addition, the precourse survey also explored students' prior experiences relevant to the CSCL task. This included their previous educational qualifications (0 = no university degree, 1 = university degree), experience with reading scientific papers (0 = no experience, 1 = some experience), familiarity with virtual learning environments (0 = unfamiliar, 1 = familiar), and previous participation in the current course (0 = first time, 1 = repeat). Similar to sociodemographic diversity, we calculated a composite score of each CSCL group's multiattributional task-related diversity ranging from 0 (complete homogeneity) to 0.5 (maximum diversity).

sections.paper_id: Voltmer et al. - 2024 - Using Topic Modeling to Research Student Diversity in Higher Education
paragraphs: The text corpus in this study consisted of 17,720 forum posts. Preprocessing steps in our study included removing numbers, punctuation, symbols, and URLs from the text, removing student names and nicknames, removing common stop words, removing tokens containing numbers, removing tokens shorter than two characters, and removing tokens appearing in fewer than five documents or fewer than five times in the entire corpus. We determined the number of topics (Atkinson-Abutridy, 2022; Grimmer et al., 2022) as follows: First, we calculated various topic models with 8-30 topics in steps of two. The fit of each model was evaluated using a combination of the intrinsic evaluation metrics semantic coherence (Mimno et al., 2011) and exclusivity (Roberts et al., 2014). The results suggested that a model with 18-22 topics would be an optimal fit. We then calculated topic models with 18-22 topics in steps of 1. A representative set of documents was examined for each topic of the five models, using human judgment to assess topic quality. Ultimately, we selected a 20-topic topic model as most appropriate for our analysis. To reduce the complexity of the analysis, we calculated the topic prevalence for each forum post, assigning the topic with the highest gamma score (i.e., the topic proportion for each document) as the main topic to each forum post.

Tutors' Group Performance Grading Four independent tutors evaluated the groups' performance using a rubric with criteria ranging from structural coherence to bibliographic accuracy and assigned scores on a scale from 0 (low quality) to 2 (high quality). The aggregate of these scores ranged from 0 to 20, representing CSCL group performance. To determine the reliability of these ratings, a small subset of wikis was independently graded by two different tutors, resulting in an acceptable inter-rater reliability, intraclass correlation coefficient = .51, CI 95 [.10, .77] (Koo & Li, 2016).

sections.paper_id: Voltmer et al. - 2024 - Using Topic Modeling to Research Student Diversity in Higher Education
Voltmer et al. - 2024 - Using Topic Modeling to Research Student Diversity in Higher Education
paragraphs: Table 1 shows a short description of the 20 topics. We labelled each topic after carefully inspecting the top frequent words (FREX) and reading representative documents for each topic. FREX (Bischof & Airoldi, 2012) represents both the frequency and exclusivity of the words associated with a topic and provides a better picture of what the topics are about and what separates them rather than focusing on the most frequent words. The 10 most important FREX terms per topic can be found in the Electronic Supplement, ESM 1. Representative documents were determined using the function "findThoughts" of the STM package for R. This function uses each topics gamma values for each document to determine representative documents for each topic. Most of the topics seemed to be task-related, e.g., Topics 1, 2, with only few notable exceptions, specifically Topics 13, 3, 5, and to some degree Topic 19. As space is limited, only the two topics most relevant to subsequent analyses, Topics 14 and 15, will be described in more detail, illustrating two topics most affected by diversity, and with high relevance to subsequent group performance. Note. % = prevalence of the topic relative to the text corpus, Groups = number of groups discussing the topic, M = average number of posts on the topic per groups relative to all groups, SD = standard deviation, Mdn = median number of posts on the topic per groups relative to all groups, Min = minimum number of posts on the topic, Max = maximum number of posts on the topic.

We labelled Topic 14 "Reporting Time Constraints to Group Members". In these forum posts, students apologized when they were not successful in completing their part of the task in time, clearly communicating to the other group members when and why they were not available, but also stating when they would continue studying, and their commitment to the group: "Unfortunately, I can't make it until Sunday evening. I'll be traveling for work all weekend." "I think I will not be able to work on the wiki this weekend. Please excuse me for that. But I will be active again on Monday." Topic 15 was labelled "Requesting Feedback from Group Members". In these forum posts, students wrote what they had changed within the shared wiki and asked the other group members to agree with the changes before they finally submitted the document, indicating the need for feedback but also making suggestions for improvement so that everyone could understand them.

sections.header_raw: Multiattributional Diversity, Topics, and Group Performance
sections.header_norm: multiattributional diversity topics and group performance
paragraphs: Table 1 shows descriptive statistics of the number of topics in forum posts. Since topic frequency represents count data, we used Spearman rank correlation coefficients, generalized linear regression analysis for Poisson distributed data using the glm function in R's stats package, and boot strapped estimates in path analyses in R's lavaan package. Topic 13 was discussed in most groups, 331, with an average of 4.23 (SD = 2.55) forum posts classified accordingly, closely followed by Topic 11, which was discussed in 317 groups and had the highest average number of posts, 7.70 (SD = 6.25). In contrast, Topic 20 was discussed in the least groups, 56, and with the least average of 0.20 (SD = 0.51) posts. Topics 1, 8, 9, 16, and 20 had a median number of posts equal to zero, indicating that those topics were discussed in less than 50% of the groups. To focus our analyses on topics relevant to the majority groups, we excluded these topics from further analyses. Average topic variety, that is, the number of topics covered by the groups, was 12.73 (SD = 3.92), indicating that the groups talked about approximately 13 of the theoretically possible 20 different topics in their forum discussions. Table 2 presents Ms, SDs, and correlations for all relevant variables. Multiattributional taskrelated diversity was positively related to Topic 12, ρ = .17, p = .002. Furthermore, both topic variety and the total number of posts written per group, as well as the number of posts classified as Topics 3,4,5,6,7,10,11,13,14,15,17,and 19 were all positively related to group performance at the end of the semester, ρs ≥ .12, ps < .05.

To examine the relationships of multiattributional diversity with the topics in subsequent group discussions, we conducted a series of path analyses. To reduce spuriousness in the estimation of direct and indirect relationships, we tested just-identified models, including all possible paths. As topic frequencies were positively correlated (see Table 2), we tested all topics in separate path models. For Topics 5 and 12, we found the two main effects of multiattributional sociodemographic diversity and multiattributional task-related diversity already indicated by correlational analyses. Moreover, and in line with our first hypothesis, interactive effects of multiattributional sociodemographic and task-related diversity were found for Topics 14 and 15. Figure 1 reports the resulting standardized and direct paths. In both models, sociodemographic diversity had a significant direct negative effect on CSCL groups' performance at the end of the semester (Topic 14: b = À0.15, 95% CI [À0.29, À0.01], z = À2.11, p = .034, β = À.10, Topic 15: b = À0.16, 95% CI [À0.31, À0.01], z = À2.18, p = .034, β = À.10), but not on the number of posts per topic, ps ≥ .13. Multiattributional task-related diversity was neither directly related to groups' performance nor the number of posts per topic, all ps ≥ .52. Supporting our first hypothesis, both multiattributional diversities had an interactive effect on the number of posts classified as Topic 14, b = À0.11, 95% CI [À0.23, À0.02], z = À2.19, p = .029, β = À.12, and Topic 15, b = À0.12, 95% CI [À0.25, À0.01], z = À2.12, p = .029, β = À.13.

In both cases, simple slopes analyses revealed that sociodemographic diversity was negatively related to the two topics in groups that also featured multiattributional taskrelated diversity, βs ≤ À.61, ps ≤ .027 (see Figure 2). Furthermore, in both models, topics were significantly and positively related to CSCL groups' performance at the end of the semester, supporting Hypothesis 2. For the indirect relationship of the interaction between multiattributional sociodemographic and task-related diversity with CSCL groups' performance via Topics 14 and 15, similar, though nonsignificant trends were visible in both path models (estimates for the indirect relationship ≤ À0.02, zs ≥ À1.77, ps ≤ .077), yielding inconclusive results regarding Hypothesis 3. Analogous path models omitting the nonsignificant Taken together, these analyses support the idea of an interactive effect of multiattributional sociodemographic and task-related diversity on specific topics, eventually affecting CSCL groups' performance.

sections.paper_id: Voltmer et al. - 2024 - Using Topic Modeling to Research Student Diversity in Higher Education
paragraphs: This is the first study using topic modeling to investigate how the content of CSCL groups' discussions is related to their multiattributional sociodemographic and task-related diversity and subsequent group performance. Studying multiattributional diversity effects is relevant for researchers and educators in online learning as the study programs' flexibility attracts heterogeneous audiences (e.g., Dutton et al., 2002;Schneller & Holmberg, 2014). Our results suggest that, if not explicitly managed, CSCL groups' multiattributional diversity may have significant and negative effects on collaboration processes among students.

In particular, the contributions of this study are twofold: First, we extracted 20 topics from a large corpus of forum posts. The topics cover a broad range of themes, some of which are socioemotional (e.g., Topic 13), while others are clearly task-related (e.g., Topics 11,6,17,5). The overall number of task-related topics is larger than the number of socioemotional topics, and the most prevalent topics are task-related, illustrating the tendency of CSCL groups to prioritize task-related over socioemotional exchange (e.g., Janssen et al., 2012;Kirschner et al., 2015). Other topics are more indirectly task-related, such as asking the other group members for feedback (Topic 15) or informing them about delays and other time constraints (Topic 14).

Evidently, not all groups use the same topics to the same extent. Hence, the second contribution of our study is the analysis of communication differences between groups with different multiattributional diversity and subsequent effects on group performance. In line with our first hypothesis, our analyses show that groups with higher multiattributional sociodemographic diversity wrote fewer forum posts concerning Topics 14 and 15 compared to groups with lower sociodemographic diversity, if groups also featured high multiattributional task-related diversity. In turn, and in line with our second hypothesis, the number of posts in these topics was positively related to independent tutors' higher grading of the groups' work. Both Topics 14 and 15 deal with elements of group coordination that are vital for a successful collaboration: Topic 14 includes discussions around time management and the commitment of group members to the study schedule. Effective time management is essential for group performance (for a brief overview, see Xu et al., 2013), especially for nontraditional students with obligations outside of the curriculum (MacCann et al., 2012). Topic 15 relates to the importance of internal group review and feedback mechanisms before finalizing work. Feedback is a critical step in ensuring quality and cohesion in group work (e.g., Oliveira et al., 2011), and it might be particularly significant in diverse groups for ensuring that all perspectives are considered, resulting in a positive relationship between these two topics and subsequent group performance. With regard to group diversity, our findings corroborate previous research indicating that the combination of high multiattributional task-related diversity and high multiattributional sociodemographic diversity presents a high-risk constellation for CSCL groups (Voltmer et al., 2024), further illuminating potential microdynamics underlying this risk constellation: In line with earlier research emphasizing the importance of a common ground and trust in virtual teams (Garrison et al., 2010;Kirschner et al., 2008;Pinjani & Palvia, 2013;van Dijk et al., 2016), Topics 14 and 15 could be interpreted as requiring more trust in fellow group members compared to other task-related topics (e.g., Topic 11, "Dividing Guiding Questions #2 CSCL Task," sample post: "What do you think if we divide the questions and the text summary? Then everyone can work on their own part"). Even Topic 19 ("Communicate Time Delays to Group Members") differs from Topic 14 in an important aspect: The sample forum posts indicate that students in Topic 19 ask for excuses for delays after the fact (e.g., "I'm sorry I'm only getting in touch so late now, as I've been ill for the last few days. I haven't been on my laptop very much, but I'm now back in full swing again [. . .].") rather than communicating transparently a priori.

Three methodological aspects make our findings particularly convincing, we believe: First, we tested relationships of diversity with the power of a large sample of over 340 randomly composed groups. We can thus rule out that our conclusions were biased by self-selection processes or small sample size. Second, we analyzed a combination of groupaveraged self-report data on group diversity, behavioral data from Moodle forums, and tutors' grading, minimizing the likelihood that our conclusions are flawed by students' selfpresentational or social desirability concerns. Third, we followed our research participants in an ecologically valid longitudinal design over a 9-week CSCL assignment.

Nevertheless, we also have to acknowledge possible limitations. First, even though our longitudinal analyses modeled the group work's chronological flow, the standard limitations of drawing causal conclusions from correlational research apply. However, the larger data set in which we tested our assumptions allowed for additional analyses to strengthen our conclusions further. Second, because of the large number of groups, only a small subset of all assignments was graded by two tutors to calculate inter-rater reliability. Even though inter-rater reliability was acceptable, it was far from perfect. Third, our analyses document significant negative relationships between diversity and the number of forum posts classified as two of the 15 topics we tested. Due to the relatively high correlation between some topics, we chose to test the direct and indirect effects of multiattributional diversity in separate models. Given the exploratory nature of our research, we decided against an α level correction. While this approach can disentangle the unique contributions of each topic and help discovering small effects, the potentially increased likelihood of a type 1 error has to be considered interpreting our results. That said, it is plausible that multiattributional diversity does not affect all topics equally. For instance, some topics were more strongly encouraged by the instructions of the CSCL task (i.e., introducing oneself to the group or dividing the task among group members). Consequently, these topics were present in most groups and less affected by diversity. Topics 14 and 15 on the other hand represent the organizing patterns by autonomous groups and their ability to function effectively and produce a collective output. Still, considering this as the major limitation of the present work, future research is needed to expand on and validate the relevance of these topics to multiattributional diverse groups in CSCL contexts.

The limitations of the present work notwithstanding, we believe that the current results have important practical implications. Many distance education contexts are inherently characterized by high levels of student diversity on several attributes (e.g., Schneller & Holmberg, 2014). Our findings show that this constellation is particularly challenging for successful CSCL due to its impact on topics relevant for subsequent group performance. A main practical implication of the present study thus concerns the active management of CSCL group diversity. Our analyses suggest that the effect of multiattributional sociodemographic diversity is particularly pronounced when knowledge and expertise are distributed unevenly in the group (i.e., when individual group members depend on each other in the solution of the task). Building on this finding, one might attempt to keep a high level of task-related interdependence among group members while devising social psychological interventions fostering the sense of a common CSCL in-group identity (e.g., Shan et al., 2021) so that sociodemographic differences move into the perceptual background. Ideally, such an intervention would create the common ground and trust necessary to sufficiently communicate time management and feedback mechanisms represented by Topics 14 and 15 in our analysis despite high levels of multiattributional student diversity. Even though our findings are consistent with such an intervention strategy, future research is needed to validate our findings and test such an intervention.
